text: Dense adhesions from the previous surgery are visible in the surgical field. The surgeon begins adhesiolysis to carefully separate these adhesions.
The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis to separate the ileum, which is densely adhered to the sigmoid colon, a common occurrence following previous surgeries involving fistula formation.
The surgeon completes the adhesiolysis to fully separate the ileum from the sigmoid colon, followed by mobilization of the bowel, preparing it for resection.
The surgeon inspects the newly formed bowel anastomosis, ensuring it is tension-free to promote proper healing and function.
The surgeon carefully separates the adhesions of the ileum from the lateral pelvic wall and the sigmoid colon, using precise techniques to free the small intestine from these dense attachments.
The surgeon completes the adhesiolysis, separating the ileum from the sigmoid colon, to enable the mobilization of the bowel in preparation for resection.
The surgeon separates the ileum from the psoas muscle, effectively removing the intestine from the pelvic region and clearing the pelvis for bowel mobilization.
The surgeon separates the bladder, which is densely adherent to the anterior aspect of the sigmoid colon, preparing to mobilize the sigmoid colon for resection.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the ureter at the sacral promontory, while separating adhesions of the parasigmoid and pararectum from the lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, entering the retro-rectal plane to mobilize the bowel while sparing nerves. The posterior aspect is relatively free of adhesions, and dissection proceeds at the plane of least adhesions. The dissection continues posteriorly to the level of the coccyx bone, followed by posterolateral dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection on the posterolateral aspect, carefully removing pararectal fat to isolate and separate the bowel from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon separates bladder adhesions from the vaginal apex, pushing the bladder downward to reveal the margin of the vault. A similar dissection is performed on the opposite side from the sacral promontory, carefully sparing the nerves.

The surgeon has mobilized the bowel away from the posterior and posterolateral aspects, revealing the location of the fistula. The vaginal apex and rectum are aligned in one plane. The dissection is performed parallel to the pararectal fat to address the five millimeter fistula identified in the MRI fistulogram.

The surgeon dissects parallel to the fat, ensuring that only the fistula remains adherent at the anterior aspect of the rectum and vagina. The rest of the bowel is mobilized away, preparing for a precise cut on the bowel.

The surgeon uses electrocoagulation to make a cut on the bowel, preparing it for resection. The bowel segment is visibly marked for removal, with the option to use a cold knife for conservation in cases like endometriosis.
The surgeon uses electrocoagulation to perforate the bowel, creating an opening that leads into the fistula. The perforation is visible as the bowel is opened.
The surgeon identifies the anterior margin of the open bowel and performs dissection below the level of the fistula, ensuring the area is prepared for the bowel resection.
The surgeon demonstrates the separation of the Danone-Willers fascia, which appears exceedingly fibrotic due to the presence of a rectovaginal fistula and associated inflammation.
The surgeon uses the fistula's opening as a guide for further dissection, revealing the opening of the vagina, which appears as a single tract.

The surgeon opens and mobilizes the rectum while keeping the vagina inside the surgical field, freeing it from adhesions. The fistula tract is visibly freed from adhesions, marking the completion of the preparation phase.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the bowel at the level of the fistula, ensuring the tissue is free from adhesions and prepared for subsequent anastomosis.

The surgeon uses electrocoagulation to cut open dense adhesions at the level of the fistula tract, allowing for complete mobilization of the fistula. The final end margins of the fistula are identified and cut, while the non-fibrotic tissue below the Denonvilliers fascia is dissected away.
The rectum is visibly separated from the vagina, with the fistula's opening clearly visible on the anterior aspect of the rectum.
